THE NEW CENTURY.

When in the dim, gray East shall rise,
	The morning of the birth—
When thy first dawn steps from the skies
	Upon the hills of earth—
Shall waiting nations breathless stand
	Oppressed with haunting fears,
Of what thou holdest in thy hand, 
	Thou coming Hundred Years.

Or shall a glad world welcome thee
	With laughter and a song
Thou unborn child of Destiny—
	Whose reign shall be so long ?
Who knows !—we only know that thou
	Shalt enter like a king
Into thy courts,—that we must bow,
	Whatever thou dost bring.

What matter whether war or peace
	Thy heralds shall proclaim—
The story of the centuries
	Is evermore the same !
Thy children-years shall tell abroad,
	Through all thy mighty span,
Naught but the Fatherhood of God—  
	The Brotherhood of Man.

JENNIE BETTS HARTSWICK.


[Column 1]
THE GREAT AND GOOD QUEEN VICTORIA. 	

The death of Queen Victoria has caused
not only sincere sorrow and deep and heart-
felt mourning in Great Britain, but there is
a universal feeling in all the civilized world
that the greatest sovereign of her time has
departed. In fact she is spoken of as the
greatest without making comparisons. Not
only queen of Great Britain and Ireland,
and colonies attached thereto, but Empress
of India. Her life reads like a fable almost,
so grand have been her virtues and so
numerous the honors that have come to her
unsought by herself. So magnificently has
she reigned that it has been hard for those
who envied her (and indeed they were few)
to find any grounds of complaint either in
her public or private life, and her name will
go down through the ages as the good, pure
and true Queen Victoria. This is the more
remarkable when one considers how very
long her reign has been, longer than any
other king or queen who has sat upon the
British throne.
	The reading public are everywhere in-
formed that her death occurred at Osborne
House, on the Isle of Wight, January 22,
6:30 a. m. The Prince of Wales dispatch to
the Lord-Mayor of London reads:
	“My beloved mother has just passed away
surrounded by her children and grandchildren.
(Signed) Albert Edward. 
The Lord-Mayor’s reply was.
“Your Imperial Highness’ telegram an-

[Column 2]
nouncing the nations great loss I have re-
ceived with profound distress and grief, and
have communicated this most sad intimation
to my fellow citizens. Her majesty’s name
and memory will forever live in the hearts of
her people.
	“May I respectfully convey to your royal
highness and to all members of the royal
family the earnest sympathy and condolence
of the city of London in your great sorrow.”
	The queen is said to have bade farewell in
a feeble monosyllable to her family assembled
at her bedside at mid-day. She first recog-
nized the Prince of Wales to whom she
spoke a few words of great moment; then
Emperor William and the others present filed
past and heard a whispered good bye. All
those in the bedroom were in tears.
	Volumes might and will undoubtedly be
written of this celebrated sovereign and most
excellent woman; but nevertheless in a great
kingdom like the British Empire order must
be preserved, and therefore another monarch,
even though he be bowed in grief, must be
proclaimed, and the regular routine observed.
	“London, January 23, 1 p. m. The King
Emperor entered his capital at 12:55 and pro-
ceeded to Marlborough House where at the
meeting of the privy council the king took
the title of Edward VII, king of the United
Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland and
emperor of India.
	After attending the first privy council at St.
James palace, at 2 p. m., when a decision will
be reached regarding the public proclamation
of his accession, which, it is expected, will be
read tomorrow at 10 a. m., his majesty will
proceed to the house of lords for the meeting
of parliament.
	London has donned a garb of mourning
from end to end and an early hour found a
thick fog enveloping the city like a pall, a
fitting accompaniment of the funeral apparel
of every one. Throughout the west end
drawn blinds are the order of the day, while
on all the embassies, government offices and
public buildings the flags are half-masted. The
law courts, the stock exchange and all the
produce and metal exchanges throughout the
country only assemble to close immediately
after the presiding officers had addressed to
the members a few words of tribute to the
dead monarch.”
 			*	*	*
	In the throne room at the St James palace
a gathering of great men, cabinet ministers,
press commoners, Bishops, Judges, the Lord-
Mayor, etc., including the Duke of York,
the Duke of Connaught and lesser members.
of the royal family, Lord Salisbury, Lord
Rosebury, A. J. Balfour, the Duke of
Devonshire, Lord Strathcona and Mount-
Royal and prominent personages to see the
king take the formal oath, binding him to
govern the kingdom according to law and
customs, and hear him assume the title of
King Edward VII. of Great Britain and
Ireland and Emperor of India. The cere-
mony was according to precedent. The Duke
of Devonshire, Lord-President of the
Council, formally communicated the death of
Queen Victoria and the succession of her
son, the Prince of Wales.
	The Lord Chancellor, Lord Halsbury, ad-

[Column 3]
ministered the oath to the king, afterwards to
the various members of the Council, com-
mencing with the Lords in Council, took the
oath of allegiance and passed in turn before 
his majesty, as at a levee, excepting that each
paused and kissed the king’s hand before
passing out.”
	The King was lustily cheered as he drove
to Marlborough House, the crowd was im-
mense. Opposite the house gates a tall
gentleman raised his hat and shouted “Long
live the king,” when the crowd cheered more
loudly than ever. The king wore a military,
uniform at the ceremonies and his speech was
brief and extemporaneous.
	The proclamation of the accession of His
Majesty was signed by the princes present,
first Duke of York, the Duke of Connaught,
the Duke of Cambridge, Prince Christian,
the Arch-Bishop of Canterbury, the Lord-
Chancellor, the Lord-Mayor and the other
representatives of the city of London.
	At 4:30 p. m. the artillery began firing
salutes in St. James park to signalize King
Edward’s accession to the throne. There
were many other public testimonials of this
royal event.
The sympathy of all nations with the peo-
ple of Great Britain in the death of their
Queen is very apparent by the marks of pub-
lic respect and reverence, business suspended
and flags at half-mast, telegrams from all
nationalities announce the fact, of the love in
which she was held and eulogies of her good-
ness and popularity fill the papers of every
land.
	When the Pope received the news of the
Queen’s death, he dropped on his knees to
pray, remaining several minutes in that atti-
tude. He gave orders that in all the churches
prayers for her majesty be offered the follow-
ing day.
								Editor.


GREAT IN SMALL THINGS.
HOW TO FORGIVE.
Keep the picture of Christ being crucified 
before you, remember these words "Father 
forgive tbem, for they know not what they 
do," or if you have been injured, you know
that you are happier than they who have
wronged you, for it is better to suffer 
wrong than to do wrong. If you were 
acquainted with the circumstances that in-
duced this person to do wrong, you would
undoubtedly say, "I am afraid that I could 
not have done so well had I been in his 
place." Tender him your sympathy and 
be thankful that you are stronger-minded 
and able to combat with temptation better
than he or she, or you have received the 
light of the Gospel, which he has not.
Criticising and finding fault will have its 
effect. When you meet that person again,
whom you have spoken against, there is a 
feeling of dislike, not love or leniency,
which we should have had.
We are all God's children, with all our 
faults and failings, and very liable to yield 
to temptation. If we are not able to do a 
great work, can we not do good in little 
things? always having leniency one with 
another, selecting the good from a person's


character and letting the bad alone; filling
our lives with so much good that the evil
will have no place whatever. By doing
this it will be said of us, as of all good peo-
ple, “I like to be with them, it does me
good, I feel better and happier in their
company, they exert a good influence.”
	‘‘Judge not that ye be not judged.”
Leave it to a higher Judge who is better
able than we are to know how to judge.
Our religion is broad and by studying these
principles of our religion we can become
great in small things. Our influence will
be felt wherever we go, we will be loved
because we have charity and live for others

			A GOOD WOMAN.

	One who on her bed of sickness was, as
she always had been, thinking of others,
had learned that an old book keeper was
out of employment, which worried her.
The Elders were called in to administer to
her. After they had gone, she said, “I am
so sorry, but I was feeling so bad while
they were here that I forgot to ask them if
they could give this brother employment.”
	The second time when they were called
in she thought of it. The employment was
not found but it was taken under advise-
ment and the brother was helped.
	She would also mention some good that
others had done her, which they undoubted-
ly had forgotten. She could most likely
have found a great deal of evil to say of
them had she been looking for it.
	Love begets love, and this was the dis-
position of this good woman, this true Saint
of God, who had all her life been gentle,
kind, sympathetic and forgiving, even to
those who had been thoughtless. Her life
was a lesson to all those who came in con-
tact with her, and they must have felt the
sweet influence of the spirit of meekness
and forbearance. To do good to others was
ever her motto in life. She has passed from
our midst, but her memory is still fresh and
green in the hearts of her children and
grandchildren and all her family, and she
will always be held in honorable remem-
brance among neighbors and friends.
	‘‘Blessed are the dead who die in the
Lord from henceforth, for their works shall
follow them.”
							R. A. S.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


VISIT IN ST. GEORGE STAKE. 
	Sister Morris and I left St. George with
the missionaries, Brothers William Stirling
and Oscar Bentley, to visit the settlements
south of St. George, November 9, 1900.
We reached Littlefield that evening and
held meeting with the Primary Association,
presided over by Sister Hannah Iverson.
This settlement is a corner of Arizona. We
go on about ten or fifteen miles and find
ourselves in Nevada.
	On the 10th we reached Bunkerville. We
held a meeting with the Primary, Sister
Harriet M. Earl (who is very efficient) pre-
siding. 	We were entertained at Bishop
Edward Bunker’s, who informed us that he
had raised 8,400 pounds of seedless dried
grapes from three acres of land. 	It shows
us what can be done by a systematic way
of working.	Bishop Bunker went, to Cali-
fornia and learned what he could in regard
to raising grapes.
	Sunday morning we met with the Sab-
bath School.	 After the exercises Brother
Stirling talked on the first principles of the
Gospel, followed by Brother Bentley. After


the afternoon meeting we met with the
Relief Society. Read the circular letter in
regard to building a house to hold our
General Conference in, in Salt Lake City.
There was a ready response to it. The
sisters feel to serve the Lord, observe the
Word of Wisdom, pay their tithes and
offerings, attend their prayers and teach
their children to pray.
On the 12th we traveled over to St.
Joseph and held a meeting in the evening.
I believe all the people of the settlement
were there. The brethren gave them the
Gospel in its purity. We had a good time.
On the 13th we went to Overton. We
held a meeting in the forenoon for the people,
in the afternoon for the Relief Society. The
Bishop met with us and we filled vacancies
in the Relief Society and Primary.
On the 14th we went on to St. Thomas,
visited among the sisters. Met in an eve-
ning meeting. Bishop Jones said we had
the spirit of the Lord in our midst.
There is plenty of good land in the
Muddy Valley, and would make homes for
many people if enough would come in to
purchase it.
On the 17th we returned to Bunkerville.
In the evening we held a meeting with the
advanced Primary, the children going
through the exercises very creditably.
On the 16th we went to Musquito Flat.
Here the people are filling up this settlement
very fast. We held two meetings here and
went on to Brother Nephi Johnson’s, where
they are building a very nice house,and par-
took of their hospitality. We arrived at
Littlefield in time to hold an evening meet-
ing.
We reached St. George on the 17th,
having traveled two hundred miles, held
twelve meetings and, we trust, done some
good in urging the sisters to their duty.
 Ann C. Woodbury.


LADIES SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.
Minntes of the Ladies’ Semi-Monthly
Meeting, Jan. 12 President M. I. Horne presid-
ing. Present on the stand, President M. I. 
Horne, Annie T. Hyde, Elizabeth Webb,
S J. Cannon, Julia C. Howe.
	Meeting commenced by singing, "All 
hail the new born year." Prayer by Sister 
Sarah Jane Cannon. Singing, "Come let 
us anew our journey pursue." 
	President Horne welcomed the sisters at 
their first semi-monthly meeting of the new 
year and the new century. Made very 
instructing remarks relative to the prophecies 
of the opening of the new year and new 
century, and concerning this people. Testi-
fied of the goodness of the Lord in assisting 
the people to reclaim the desert places and 
improve this country to which we were led 
by the prophets of the Lord. Prayed for 
the spirit of the Lord on all the people.
	A sister in the congregation testified to 
the goodness of the Lord to her in restoring 
her memory and in answering her prayers 
in many instances. Sister Julia C. Howe
testified of the goodness of the Lord to her.
Was grateful for the preservation of her 
life. Felt that there was a great work for 
her to do in working for her dead. Ac-
knowledged the goodness of the Lord in 
answering her prayers. 
	Sister Sarah J. Cannon considered it a 
privilege to attend these meetings, for the 
words of testimony uttered were a great 
help to her. Hoped we would all live ex-
emplary lives and live so that we would be 
saved in the celestial kingdom.
[column 2]
	Sister Webb thought these meetings 
created a feeling of love among those who
attended. Hoped we would all live so as to 
have faith to meet the trials and scourges 
that were bound to be in our midst and of 
which we will be sure to have a part.
	A sister in the congregation from the 
Twenty-Fourth Ward bore her testimony. 
Sister Annie T. Hyde encouraged the 
sisters to speak, as it not only assisted 
themselves, but helped others. Spoke of 
the necessity of doing Temple work and of 
the great development of science in the past 
century, was sure we could not have an 
idea of what awaited us in the future. Bore 
a faithful testimony to the truth of the 
Gospel.
	A sister in the congregation bore an 
earnest testimony.
	Sister Sophia T. Nuttall said she was 
glad to be present, was especially thankful
for health and the preservation of her life 
and the lives of her children. Felt to praise 
her Heavenly Father and to rejoice in 
the work, and hoped to be able to endure to 
the end.
	Sister Brown thanked her Heavenly 
Father for all the blessings given her, was 
thankful for the privilege of working in the 
Temple. 
A sister from Brighton Ward realized 
that it is a great blessing to live at this 
time, hoped to have strength to do the 
labor devolving on her. Had received 
many testimonies of the truth of the Gos-
pel.
	A sister in the congregation said she 
knew the Lord would open the way for us if 
we earnestly ask him to assist us, made 
other interesting remarks.
	Sister Hardy prayed for strength to re-
main faithful and for assistance to perform 
the duties of life. Was thankful for the 
blessings of the Lord.
	A sister spoke of the blessings of the 
Father to her in giving her a testimony of 
the truth. 
	Sister Mary Silver was glad to be a mem-
ber of the Church, thought we should 
stand firm and keep the commandments 
given us and the covenants we have made. 
	Sister Clarissa S. Williams bore her testi-
mony to the blessings of the Lord which 
are constantly given to His people.
	A sister in the congregation spoke of the 
blessings which she had received from the
Lord.
	Another sister said she had been in the 
Church fifty years. She had a testimony
that this was the work of the Lord. 
Sister Barrow was glad to have a testi-
mony. She was always learning something.
Spoke of the answer to her prayers in giv-
ing health and strength to her children. 
Knew that the Lord did answer prayers,
We must work for the blessings we receive.
	Sister Horne made a few closing remarks.
Said the sisters should study the life of the 
Savior, and we should do as we would like 
to be done by. Spoke of administering to 
the sick, said that those administering 
should have the spirit of the Lord, or the 
sympathy of the one administering might 
impel him to promise blessings which the 
Lord did not see fit to give.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Sing-
ing, Doxology. 	Benediction by Sister
Silver.
	CLARISSA S. WILLIAMS, Sec. pro tem.


HON. GEORGE F. HOAR is still the
senior Senator from Massachusetts.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE POET.

His home is on the heights; to him
Men wage a battle weird and dim;
Life is a mission stern as fate,
And Song a dread apostalate. 
The toils of prophecy are his,
To hail the coming centuries—
To ease the steps and lift the load 
Of souls that falter on the road.
The perilous music that he hears
Falls from the voices of the spheres.

He presses on before the race.
And sings out of a silent place, 
Like faint dotes of a forest bird
On heights afar that voice is heard;
And the dim path he breaks today
Will some time be a trodden way.
But when the race comes toiling on,
That voice of wonder will be gone—
Be heard on higher peaks afar,
Moved upward with the morning star.

O, men of earth, that wondering voice 
Still goes the upward way; rejoice!

SELECTED.


[Column One]
A TRIBUTE OF LOVE.
SISTER JANE BALLANTYNE TAYLOR.

A mother in Israel has gone to her rest,
	She has said her farewell to loved ones so dear, , 
Across the bright river in realms of the blest,
	She was welcomed by those who loved her while
		here.

Dear friends, do not sorrow, her trials are o’er,
She has fought the good fight, the battle is won,
Anon you will meet her on that golden shore,
And she’ll joyfully echo the plaudit, “Well
		done.”

	For such women as Sister Jane Taylor
there is joy unspeakable in meeting those
hear ones who have gone before, but to
those who remain the loss is great of the
presence in the home. The angelic spirit
that characterized her whole life was more
than an example, it was like sweet incense
the household. She has gone from her
earthly home, we shall hear her sweet voice
no more, nor see her smile of welcome.
	Yet we can never forget her saintly man-
ner and loving spirit, always the same gentle
welcome to her friends.
	As she lay in her white robes in the
beautiful casket, she wore the same sweet
smile and looked like a bride going to the
marriage altar. The floral tributes of
loving children, grandchildren and friends
seemed as fitting for this queenly mother as
though she were indeed going on a wedding
tour. The purity and integrity of her long
life are a bright example to the young
women of Zion.
			 ---------
Sister Jane Ballantyne Taylor was born
April 11, 1813, in Sheffield, near Edinburg,
Scotland. Her father was David Ballantyne
and her mother Annie Bannerman Ballan-
tyne. Richard Ballantyne, famous in
Sunday School work in early days in the
valley, and until his demise, not long ago,
was her brother. The Ballantyne family
embraced the Gospel in Scotland in 1842.
	In 1843 they emigrated to America and
were seven weeks in crossing the ocean to
New Orleans. They went direct to Nauvoo
and February 25, 1844, Miss Ballantyne be-
came the wife of the late President John
Taylor. The marriage ceremony was per-
formed by Patriarch Hyrum Smith. Sister
Taylor shared in all the trials, hardships and
sufferings of the troublous times. At the time
of the exodus irom Nauvoo she crossed the
Mississippi river, walking over it the whole
distance, when at every step the ice cracked
under her feet. The weather was intensely
cold, the Saints were not well provided with
shelter and Sister Taylor, with another

[Column Two]
sister, sat up in a wagon the whole night, as
there was not room to lie down, but no
murmur escaped her lips. During the
arduous journey from Nauvoo to the Mis-
souri river, and the prolonged stay at
Winters Quarters, she, in common with
others, bad much to endure, but her faith
never wavered, she was willing to endure
the trials to gain her experience, and to
earn an exaltation hereafter.
	Her faith in the Gospel was firm and
steadfast, never doubting the goodness of
God, always hopeful and patient under
even the severest trials.
	President Taylor and family came across
the plains from the Missouri river to the
Salt Take Valley in 1847, in the first com-
pany behind the pioneers, so that Sister Jane
Taylor was a real pioneer.
	Annie, her eldest child, was born in
October, 1849, just as her husband was
leaving to go on amission to France, and
the family were very scantily provided for.
But Sister Taylor with her sublime faith
and trust in God was nothing daunted, and
then, as always, thanked God for His good-
ness and trusted on. A noble, heroic
woman, in every way worthy the name of
Saint.
	Her youngest son, David John, died very
suddenly while in New York on business in
the summer of 1881, leaving a young wife
and babe. His mother bore this trial as
she had all others, with heroic complacency,
acknowledging the hand of God even in
this seemingly lamentable occurrence. She
leaves one son, Richard B. Taylor, who
resides in Ogden, and one daughter, Annie,
wife of Alonzo E. Hyde, of this city, many
grandchildren and three great grandchildren.
It was at her daughter’s residence that she
passed away, having resided there ever
since her husband’s death in 1887. Her
children, Richard and Annie, and her eldest
grandchildren were wiih her to the last.
She fell asleep without a struggle on the
morning of Tuesday, December 26, 1900,
at the age of eighty-seven years eight
months and fifteen days. Sister Taylor
leaves one sister almost her own age, Sister
Annie Quayle, of Ogden.
	“Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord,
yea, from henceforth, for their works follow
them.”
	Sister Taylor was a woman of generous
impulses and gave much to the needy, and.
she was especially charitable in heir estimate
of the character of others. It is said of her
that she never spoke evil of any one; silence
was her habit when there was gossip or
islander of one’s character, unless she could
refute what was being said. In reviewing
her life, her virtues shine out most conspicu-
ously, though she was of a very retiring
disposition, the old-fashioned type, as
Milton says in “Paradise Lost,”

	“Nothing lovelier can be found.
In woman than to study household good,
	And good works in her husband to promote.”
				---------
	The funeral services were held Thusday,
December 28, at the residence of her
daughter, Bishop George H. Taylor presid-
ing. The opening hymn, “I need Thee
every hour,” was sweetly rendered by a
quartette of male voices. Prayer was offered
by Elder Rodney C. Badger, and the next
hymn very feelingly sung was “Nearer my
God to Thee.”
	The remarks made by the speakers were
appropriate and telling. Bishop Taylor
made opening remarks followed by Presi-
dent Angus M. Cannon and Apostle John

[Column Three]

W. Taylor. Then Mrs. Maggie Hull sang
sympathetically, in her clear voice, without
accompaniment, the good old hymn, “I
know that my Redeemer lives.” After-
wards Apostle John Henry Smith and
President Joseph F. Smith made very
consoling and impressive remarks, and
the quartette sang “We shall meet Beyond
the River.” Benediction by Counselor
Arthur Barnes of the Bishopric of the ward.
A large number of people, relatives and
friends followed the deceased to her last
resting place in the city cemetery. The
grave was dedicated by Elder John W.
Taylor.

E. B. W.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	"Dr. Alice Byram Condict, of Orange,
N. J., after serving eight years as a self-
supporting medical missionary in India, is
how doing medical missionary work m
Manila. She left Bombay three months
ago after exhausting herself caring for the
famine sufferers.”

	Miss Mary G. Hay, who has been for
some years one of the ablest and most
efficient organizers in the ranks of the
National Suffrage Association, has accepted
an important position in the Mutual Life
Insurance Company, where her admirable
business talents will have large scope.

	Miss Estelle Reel, Superintendent of
Indian Education, has just issued her
annual report, from which the following
extracts are made. Since her appointment
26 months ago, Miss Reel has been in the
field 17 months, lias inspected 49 schools,
traveled 41,138 miles, of which 2,087 miles
were covered by wagon, pack horse and
on foot, over lofty mountains, through
dense forests, on remote frontiers and over
rugged trails between precipitous cliffs. 
	Col. T. W. Higginson, accompanied
by Mrs. Higginson, has gone on a two
weeks lecturing tour out West It is many
years now since he has given lectures in
cities outside Boston and vicinity. He
does it now because he feels physically
able. He will speak in Chicago before the
Twentieth Century Club, in Cleveland at
the Northwestern University, offering three
lectures on “American orators and ora-
tory,” and in Minneapolis, Indianapolis
and Akron, O He has also in view a trip
to the Mediterranean, taking in Grenada, 
the Alhambra and Italy, and returning by
way of France and England. His wife and
daughter accompany him. They will sail 
in March.

	Mrs. Julia Ward Howe was an honored
guest at the banquet of the Daughters of
Vermont on the last night of the old year,
and was introduced by the president of the
club, Mrs. Sallie Joy White, as one of
Massachusetts’, America’s and the world’s
loved daughters. Mrs. Howe referred
pleasantly to her visits to Vermont in win-
ter when it was sheathed in ice, and in
summer when it was blooming and green,
and it was always beautiful. She said she
had lived four-fifths of the century just
closing. The country had grown and 
cities sprung up like flowers since she
could remember. “Let us expect the new
century will surpass the one now going
out,” she said. ‘‘Let us expect that all
good things will go forward and surround
[]he world with a bond of p


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

Woman's Exponent.

EMMELINE B. WELLS, Editor and Publisher.

	Published semi-monthly, in Salt Lake City, Utah.
Terms: one copy one year, $1.00; one copy six months,
50 cts. No reduction made for clubs. City papers deliv-
ered by mail, extra for postage one year, 25 cts.
	Advertising rates: Each square, ten lines of nonparie
	ace one time $2.50; per month, $3.00. A liberal di-
count to regular advertisers.
	EXPONENT office Room 208 Templeton, South Temple
Street: Business hours from 10 a. m. to 5 p. m., every
day, except Sunday.
	Address all business communications to
Mrs. E. B. WELLS,

econd-class matter.

SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JAN. 15, & FEB. 1, 1901.


[Column 1]
SOME HAPPENINGS OF THE NEW YEAR.
	President George Q. Cannon’s visit to the
Sandwich Islands to attend the Semi-Centen-
nial celebration of the advent of the Gospel
into that faroff land, was an event of consider-
able significance, and as President Cannon
was one of that company of Elders, though
then a very young man, who first took the
Gospel to that people fifty years ago, it did
seem a fitting tribute to him, that he should
be present and give them the opportunity to
hear him speak in the language he learned at
that time, and it must have been a great
satisfaction to them to see and hear him.
	Those who have read the account of
his trip in the Deseret Evening News know
how delighted the native people were, and
especially the descendants of those who re-
ceived the Elders into their homes at that
time, to have him among them now that he
has attained to such a high position in the
Church and in the nation.
	It was very affecting even to read of the
ovation paid him, and must have been a
most pathetic scene to witness. It reminds
one very forcibly of Longfellow’s salutation to
his college fifty years after, when the class in
Bowdoin college celebrated its semi-centen-
nial. The poem is one of the most beautiful
in the English language.
**Alas, what have I said?
What greetings come there from the voiceless dead?
What salutation, welcome or reply?
What pressure from the hands that lifeless lie?
They are no longer here; they all are gone
Into the land of shadows—all save one.
Honor and reverence, and the good repute
That follows faithful service as its fruit,
Be unto him, whom living we salute.

The great Italian poet, when he made
His dreadful journey to the realms of shade
Met there the old instructor of his youth
And said in tones of pity and of ruth:
“O never from the memory of my heart
Your dear, paternal image shall depart,
Who while on earth, ere yet by death surprised.
Taught me how mortals are immortalized.
***
Today we make the poet’s words our own,
And utter them in plaintive undertone;
Nor to the living only be they said,
But to that other living called the dead,
Whose dear paternal images appear
Not wrapped in gloom but robed in sunshine here;
Whose simple lives complete and without flaw,
Were part and parcel of great Nature’s law,
Who said not to their Lord, as if afraid
"Here is thy talent in a napkin laid,”
But labored in their sphere, as men who live
In the delight that work. alone can give.
Peace be to them, eternal peace and rest,
And the fulfillment of the great behest;
“Ye have been faithful over a few things,
Over ten cities shall ye reign as kings,”

[Column 2]
	Since January 1st of this year, 1901,
time has been busy with its record of notable
happenings. The inauguration of the
Governor of Utah and the State officials
should be mentioned first perhaps, although
the demonstrations were unostentatious, it
was sort of military, Brigadier General John
Q. Cannon, of the National Guard of Utah,
having charge of the affair.
	The official proceedings took place in the
Hall of Representatives, in the City and
County Building, the platform being
appropriately and artistically draped with
flags and banners, the Declaration of
Independence, handsomely framed, hanging
on the wall at the back and over the heads
of the judges and officers of the State.
Held’s Baud discoursed martial music.
General Cannon was Master of Ceremonies.
	Prayer was offered by the Right Reverend
Bishop Scanlan, of the Catholic Church of
this city. Chief Justice Miner, of the
Supreme Court of Utah, administered the
oath of office in the formal manner to the
State officers, except the Governor, who
then proceeded to give his inaugural address,
and after the conclusion of the address,
which was a very able document, and when
the applause had subsided, the Chief Justice
administered the oath to the Governor, and
General Cannon announced the Governor of
Utah and gave the signal for a salute of
seventeen guns. The cannon boomed just
at the hour of twelve and the band followed
with “America,” the audience rising in re-
sponse to the national air, and the great and
grand ceremonies of State were over.
	Congratulations and handshaking fol-
lowed. The day was very stormy but all
passed off pleasantly nevertheless. Better
storms without than storms within, and one
cannot help feeling it a good omen to open
a new century so auspiciously as
Utah has by ushering in its new officers of
State with such general good feeling by all
political parties and the people concerned in
its welfare.
	Elaborate preparations had been made for
an inaugural ball to finish the day’s exer-
cises, and the Salt Lake Theatre had been
decorated profusely with flags, banners,
bunting and colored electric lights; an
immense bee hive, the State emblem con-
spicuously placed in the centre of the first
circle, opposite to the platform, added much
to the attractive features.
	Many mottoes were arranged in the colors
of lights and were very handsome, as well
as significant. The ball was given by “the
National Guard of Utah in honor of the
Governor, Heber M. Wells and the State
officers of Utah.” It was a superb affair,
and most enjoyable. Printed programs,
on the cover the bee hive and monogram of
the N. G. U. in blue and gold, encircled by
the sego lily (the State flower) and below the
words “Inaugural Ball,” with arrows
crossed underneath and finished with the
figures, 1901.
	The Governor’s staff and officers of the
National Guard were in uniform, which
added greatly to the attractions of the
scene on the floor, although, of course, the
Governor himself was the great attraction,
not even the brilliant ladies in their elabor-
ate toilettes, outshining him on this festive
occasion.

	The Legislature of the State of Utah con-
vened on Monday, January 14, and have
been in regular session since that time.
An effort was made, as there were no

[Column 3]
women members, to get the Senate to
appoint a woman chaplain, but it was not
successful.
	The electing of a United States Senator
has occupied the attention of the general
public, as naturally it should, and feeling
that matter to be a most important one,
finally, after voting for this and that candi-
date, the Republican members of the
Legislature in joint session, on Wednesday
at high noon, January 23, elecedt Hon.
Thomas Kearns United States Senator for
Utah, with the entire vote of the House
and Senate, thirty-seven in all. The
Democrats, true to their party, honored
A. W. McCune by casting the whole num-
ber of their votes for him. A committee
of two was appointed from both the Senate
and the House to conduct the Governor
and one of the Supreme Court Judges to
Senator Kearns, and present him to the
joint assembly.
	While awaiting the arrival of Senator
Kearns, Senator Smoot reminded the
Legislature that they had been notified of
the death of the greatest sovereign the
world had ever known, and thought it
proper for the Legislature to adjourn in
honor of Queen Victoria, and offered
Resolutions of sympathy.
	At 1:25 the Governor came into the hall
and introduced Senator Kearns, who briefly
addressed the Members of the Legislature
and Friends.
	“No man could receive so high an honor
as that you have just conferred upon me
without feeling a deep sense of gratitude.
To say that I thank you from the bottom
of my heart but feebly expresses my feel-
ings at this time. I am no orator. I am
but a plain, blunt man, and what I lack in
oratory I hope to make up in action. I
promise you now that so far as lies in my
power I shall do everything possible to
maintain the honor and integrity of our
beloved State, and my hig[-]est ambition
will be gratified if I can advance Utah’s
material interests and place her in the
forefront of western States. Again I thank
you.”
	The Governor said in part:
	“I have known Mr. Kearns for many
years and found him to be an example of
integrity, good citizenship and splendid
executive ability; He has risen from the
pick and drill, from what might be called
the bucket brigade, to his present high
commercial standing by his own efforts.
I have no fear as to the results of his
election as Senator. I do not fear that
that there will be blood on the moon or
that the earth will stop revolving on its
axis. He may not be long on oratory, but
he will make up for this lack in his other
sterling qualities, and I have no doubt will
prove a credit to our State.”
	Justice Miner of the Supreme Court then
said a few words on the choice of the new
Senator. He said that he believed in the
selection that had been made we would
have for Senator a business man of sterling
quality and a man of character who would
reflect honor on the State-


MRS. EVANGELINE HEARTZ, member of
the Colorado House of Representatives,
has been made chairman of the committee
on enrollment and a member of the com-
mittee on appropriations and expenditures,
education, State institutions, temperance
and public health.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	A WORD OF LOVE AND GREETING.

TO THE OFFICERS AND MEMBERS OF THE
	RELIEF SOCIETY OF THE CHURCH OF
	JESUS CHRIST OF LATTER-DAY SAINTS.

Dearly beloved Sisters, both far and near:

	With hearts full of gratitude and bless-
ings, we desire to offer you a few of our
thoughts and reflections concerning the
great work in which we, as daughters of our
Heavenly Father, are privileged to be en-
gaged.
	Were circumstances such as to permit of
our doing so, we should greatly rejoice in
visiting personally every branch of our
society, in speaking words of encourage-
ment, and where necessary offering sugges-
tions, and, perhaps, instructions, and in
shaking hands with every member; and
even the young ladies and the little chil-
dren who are to continue the work after us.
But in as much as it is impossible for us to
do this, we have decided to express our-
selves by means of writing. That you
may understand that our hearts are ever
full of loving appreciation of your faithful
and far-reaching labors and of prayers and
blessings for you all.
	To begin with, we bear you our testi-
mony that the work we are engaged in is
of God. That the Church of Jesus Christ
of Latter-day Saints and the Relief Society,
which is a portion of that Church, were
organized by the Prophet of the Lord. That
we knew Joseph Smith personally, and saw
and heard him many times speaking to the
Saints when he was so filled with the Spirit
of the Lord that his countenance became
transparent, and he looked and spoke like a
heavenly being. And the Holy Ghost bore
witness to us that Joseph Smith was a true
prophet of the living God.
	We desire to leave this testimony with
you all, and to have you understand and re-
member that the Prophet of the Lord organ-
ized our Relief Society that we might have the
glorious privilege of doing the same kind of
work that our Lord and Savior did when
He was upon this earth.
	We all know that he was interested in
looking after the sick and the afflicted, the
poor and the needy, the deaf and the dumb,
the blind and all who were discouraged or
in any way troubled. We should do all
these things cheerfully, according to the
beautiful example He has set for us, with
patience and long suffering, such as con-
tinued with Him to the last.
	Questions are often asked concerning the
requisite of a Relief Society president and
other officers of the organization. We may
answer that the holding of the sacred posi-
tion of President over a Relief Society is a
very responsible calling. It means much
besides the honor that may be attached to it.
It means constant care and solicitude, as
well as actual labor with head and hands
to keep all matters pertaining to the society
straight and right; to see that there is no
suffering of any kind in any quarter, over
which she has supervision, that there is a
possibility of relieving.
	When a sister accepts the position of
president over a Relief Society; she should
endeavor to fully consider the importance of
that position. The charitable, loving,
Gospel feelings which she must cherish to-
wards her counselors, the other officers and
all members of the Society. And the coun-
selors should be one with the president, al-
ways sustaining and upholding her in their
various duties. The president should als<o>

[Column 2]
respect her counselors, consulting with
them freely, the three being truly united, 
working in harmony, dividing responsibili-
ties and labors so that none shall be over-
taxed where it can be avoided, ever remem-
bering the Savior’s words to His disciples,
“Be ye one.”
	“He that thinketh he is something when
he is nothing, deceiveth himself.” This
passage of scripture expresses a sentiment
which we should never lose sight of. For
we are all as nothing, except the Spirit of
the Lord enlightens and helps us. If we
have not that Spirit to enable us to under-
stand and perform our duties we are not
prepared for Relief Society work; neither
can we become useful in the kingdom of
God in any capacity without the companion-
ship and assistance of His Holy Spirit. We
want to bear in mind constantly that “of
ourselves we are nothing.” We are but
frail instruments in God’s hands to assist in
His work.
	Never feel that one is of superior worth to
her neighbor, but that each is necessary in
her place. Those who hold office should
be more humble even than others. Who
should set the example of humilty, long-
suffering and charity for the failings of
others in the home but the mother? And
who in the Relief Society but the president?
And as with the president so with the
counselors, and indeed, all the other officers.
	And then: every member in the society
should consider it her duty and privilege to
uphold and assist by every means at her
disposal, the officers of the society to which
she belongs; just as a loving, faithful
daughter in the home should seek to relieve
the heavily burdened mother, as much as
possible, of her anxieties and re-
sponsibilities.
	The sisters should meet together often,
and a good, reliable record should be kept
of all that is done. A competent Secretary
is a most important factor in a society. The
Treasurer’s office is also a weighty one, and
must be filled by careful selection.
	And the teachers—oh what a responsible
position the teachers hold! How much
they require the Spirit of the Lord to help
them in their duties! Their visits to the
sisters must ever be in all kindness, love
and sympathy, to comfort, encourage and
relieve all suffering ones. Wisdom and
discretion should be among their gifts.
	These requisites, which we have but
briefly mentioned are some of the qualifica-
tions necessary to make capable officers in
the Relief Society. It is not to be supposed
that when appointed to office every sister
will be fully prepared to undertake her
duties without the probability of making
mistakes. But we must be willing to
learn and never be discouraged because of
some failures.
	The officers of a society must consult to-
gether, instructing and edifying each other
so that all may come to understand the re-
quirements of their positions.
	And here we will say, that misunder-
standings and disagreements which may arise
in the society, the sisters should settle in all
kindness, quietly and effectually among
themselves. They are an organized society
and should have the power and grace to ad-
just their own difficulties. Where they lack
wisdom, let them ask of God, who giveth
liberally and upbraideth not, and it will be
given.
	Among our young people, a knowledge
of the design of our Relief Society work
should be made more general than it is,

[Column 3]
Our children, sons as well as daughters
should be encouraged to visit our meetings
at least occasionally, that they may better
understand the purposes of this organization
to which their mothers belong.
	Hoping, dear sisters, that in the near
future we shall have the opportunity of
communicating further with you on these
most important subjects, we will now con-
clude by again extending to you our heart-
felt love and blessings, ever praying for
your welfare and happiness and the con-
tinued success and growth of our mutual
work.
	Your sincere sisters and colaborers,
				ZINA D. H. YOUNG,
				JANE S. RICHARDS,
				BATHSHEBA W. SMITH,
				SARAH J. CANNON.


NATIONAL LIVE STOCK CONVENTION.
	Great preparations were made in the
city for the National Live Stock Conven-
tion, held here the third week in January.
Business houses and all public buildings
were profusely decorated, and the Electric
Light Company did much to enhance the
beauty of the night with the colored
lights ingeniously strung here and there.
The Salt Lake Assembly Hall, in which
the Convention was held, was a dazzling
scene of splendor by night and more elabor-
atety decorated than ever before, the
heads and horns of cattle making it very
picturesque, and the seals of all the States
arranged around the gallery, were very
interesting indeed.
	The reception at the Knutsford Tuesday
evening the 15th, was a grand affair, both
for strangers and citizens. The Governor
and a few of the ladies committee (his
staff, and Gen Cannon of the N. G. U., in
uniform) received the guests in the center
of the parlor, which was very handsomely
and elaborately decorated in honor of the
ladies and gentlemen of the Convention.
Other rooms were beautifully decorated as
well as the dancing hall, which was a scene
of beauty. The music was very fine and
the evening passed pleasantly, notwith-
standing the immense crowd.
	Much credit is due the General Commit-
tee, of which the Governor was chairman,
and Mrs. Holmes, who furnished all the
beautiful flowers that made the rooms so
attractive.
	The receptions in private residences
given to the ladies of the party, were all
very pleasant affairs,and one hopes a happy
remembrance of Salt Lake City to these
lady visitors from all parts of the United
States.
	On Friday afternoon, January 18, Mrs.
Thompson and other ladies at the Mayor’s
residence, Mrs. McCornick and ladies at her
elegant home, and Mrs. Snow at the Bee
Hive, with a few of her friends,opened their
homes from 2 to 5 to the visitors of the
party making the occasion as festive as such
formal entertainments can be by furnishing
the best of music and appropriate drapery
and decorating for the time and occasion.
	At the Bee Hive, which is a historic house
built early in the settlement of Salt Lake
City, a program of the songs and music had
been printed, and each guest was given one
as a souvenir of the occasion. Some of the
best talent of the city gave both vocal and
instrumental selections from the greatest
composers.
	So many things have transpired during


the first month of the new year that it is
impossible to do justice to the subject un-
less one had more space or a daily paper.


78					WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[column 1]
	    R. S AND P. A. REPORTS.

		MARICOPA STAKE,

	The Relief Society conference of the
Maricopa Stake was held in the
Mesa Relief Society hall, Decem-
ber 20, 1900, Stake President Mabel Ann
Hakes presiding. Meeting opened with the
usual exercises, at 10 a. m. and 2 p. m.,
attendance of thirty-four officers besides the
members.
	The wards were all represented and re-
ported with one exception. The instruc-
tions were timely and such as would elevate
mankind. The subjects mostly dwelt upon
were the moral lives of ourselves and chil-
dren; reverence for the Gospel; the duties
of the Relief Society, both financially and
spiritually; restitution should not be put off
until tomorrow, also many strong testi-
monies were borne to the divinity of the
Gospel. We had a glorious conference. Ad-
journed for three months.
				ANNA E. FULLER, Sec.

		PUEBLO, COLORADO.

	The Relief Society of Pueblo, Colorado
celebrated the second anniversary of the
organization, on Tuesday, December 4,
1900. It had been organized two years on
July 3, but on account of sickness and
deaths, we concluded to put it off for
awhile. The sisters arranged a program to
be followed by refreshments. The admit-
tance was by invitation, and the hall was
quite comfortably filled.
	President Lizzie Hart welcomed the
guests, of whom a great number do not be-
long to our faith but attend meetings
regularly, we had given them invitations
and President Hart gave each gentleman a
white buttonhole Bouquet souvenir.
	First Counselor Amanda Coleman took
charge of the program. The sisters whose
names were on the program all did their
parts well. Several of the brethren spoke
and said they were certainly surprised at
the growth of the society, which now num-
bers three times as many as when it was
organized; also our Bishop, John T. Hart
was present and he spoke briefly on the
organization of our Relief Society and gave
us some good instructions which were very
encouraging to the sisters. While refresh-
ments were being laid on the tables by
Counselor Lillie Coleman, assisted by
Treasurer Turner, John Sessions and Sister
Sarah Hall, President Harts's son, Willie,
entertained the company with selections on
the phonograph. After the tables were
spread with every bounty that heart could
desire the guests were waited upon by three
charming young sisters, Martha and Maud
Hall and Bessie Coleman, assisted by
Brother Carl Frank Coleman, James T.
Johnson and Claude Betenburger.
	After supper there was singing by the
company, and they each laid every care
aside and expressed themselves as feeling
that it was the most enjoyable affair that
they had attended, which greatly encouraged
the sisters of the society, as every one had
worked faithfully to make it a success.
	Benediction was pronounced and all left
for their homes feeling happy.
AMANDA COLEMAN, Asst. Sec.
[column 2]
	PRIMARY CONFERENCE.

	Minutes of Primary Conference of the
Salt Lake Stake, held in the Assembly
Hall, December 15, 1900, President Cam-
illa C. Cobb presiding.
	On the stand with President Cobb were
her counselors, Lydia A. Wells and Mary
L. Morris and aids, President Louie B.
Felt, Counselor Lillie T. Freeze and May
Anderson, secretary of the General Board.
	Meeting opened by singing "Open the
door for the children." Prayer by Master
James Hooper. Singing, "Did you think
to pray." Roll called, twenty-one Presidents
responding with sentiments. Minutes of
previous conference read and approved.
The program was carried out as follows:
"Story of the Life of the Savior," by
the Nineteenth ward Primary.
	Song by Edith Grant, "A Miracle."
"The Widow of Nain" was repeated by
Master Pruette Goddard.
	Song, "Think of These Things," by
members of the Ninth Ward Primary.
	Recitation by Mercy Bywater, and song
by Katie Martin of the same ward.
	An address on the duties of the children
was given by Master Woolley.
	The parable, "The lillies of the field,"
was repeated by Mary Grant.
	An exercise on the "Book of Mormon"
was given by members of the Twenty-Fourth
Ward Primary.
	Song by Leah Ashton.
	Recitation and song, "The Rosebud,"
by four little girls from the Third Ward.
	Sister Olive Derbidge told a pleasing
Christmas story, which was very much en-
joyed by the children, after which Master
Asper gave a selection on the organ.
	President Felt then addressed the confer-
ence. Said she had listened with pleasure
to the exercises. Told the children that if
they wanted to be happy on Christmas day
they should visit the poor and the families
of missionaries, and try to make them hap-
py. Said as there were so many sick in
our midst would give them a mission to
pray for them.
	Veda Jones gave a recitation, entitled,
"Uncle Pete's Plea."
Counselor Lillie T. Freeze spoke to the
children. Asked them to carry out in their
lives the sentiment, "Whatsoever ye would
have others do to you, do ye even so to
them," and make it their life's lesson; and
also told them they should try to make
some one happy on Christmas day. Felt to
bless teachers and children.
	After singing, "We thank Thee, O God,
for a prophet," conference was adjourned
for three months. Benediction by Brother
Goddard.
	At the close of the conference packets of
candy were distributed to the children.
				ELLA W. HYDE, Sec.

	FRISCO RELIEF SOCIETY.

	In obedience to a call from Stake Presi-
dent of the Relief Society of Beaver
Stake, Sister C. C. Shepherd, the sisters
and a fair showing of the brethren met in
the Frisco school house August  1900, at
9:30 a. m., with a view of organizing a
society.
	Elder William Ashworth presided. The
congregation sang "We thank Thee, O
God, for a prophet." Brother S. A. Chase
offered the opening prayer. Singing,
"Redeemer of Israel."
Brother Ashworth spoke of organization
[column 3]
and reminded us that we were here to do
good, then turned the meeting into the
hands of the sisters.
	President Shepherd was pleased to see so
many present, but would have been better
pleased if there had been more. Said they
were instructed to come and organize, if the
people wanted. a Relief Society, thought
much good could be done here. "If the
sisters will do their duty the Lord will
bless them and they can do a great deal of
good."
Brother Ashworth put a vote to find how
many were willing to organize, and all were
favorable.
	Then came the question of a president
and Sister Adams nominated Sister Matilda
Olsen. A vote was finally taken and Sister
Olsen unanimously sustained.
	Comments were made and a vote taken
that we have one organization and hold
meetings alternately here and at the mine.
	Sister Olsen chose as counselors Sister
M. E. Adams and Sister Crassiah Jones.
	A secretary was called for, but the matter
was deferred. Sister Margaret Jones was
chosen treasurer.
	Matilda Olsen was then set apart as
president, Sister M. E. Adams as first
counselor, Sister Crassiah Jones as second
counselor and Sister Margaret Jones as
treasurer.
	It was then moved and seconded that
Sister Clara Griffith be secretary. Carried.
She was set apart.
	Sister M. A. White spoke, showing how
we should love each other and our God.
True happiness comes of God. The Lord
has always compassion with the sinner.
Our heavenly Father is the only judge. We
must exert our energies that we may fulfill
our duties. Was pleased with the election
of the president. People of energy are al-
ways busy. We must be wide awake
mothers and try to make our children better
than ourselves. Try to realize the responsi-
bility of motherhood. Spoke at length on
cheerfulness and control of self. Instil
truth and purity into our children. We
have duties to the sick and distressed.
Prayed God to bless our organization.
	Congregation sang "There is beauty all
around." Benediction by Brother William
Ashworth.
			SARAH GRIFFITH, Sec.


OBITUARY.
SISTER SARAH S. HARDER.
 	Sister Sarah S. Harder died at her residence in
Kamas, Summit Co., October 28, 1900, after a long
and painful illness and severe suffering for five
months.
	She was born in Portsmouth, England, March 3,
1840, and was the daughter of Stephen and Eliza-
beth Biddlecome Spicer, and the wife of [-]illet S.
Harder. She leaves a husband, two married
daughters, seven grandchildren and many friends
to mourn her loss. Two sons preceded her to the
great beyond. She was a faithful member of the
Kamas Relief Society for many years.
	Sister Harder was set apart as assistant secretary
on March 7, 1881, and appointed secretary of the
same society on June 10, 1895, which position she
held until her death. Her Relief Society work
was well and faithfully done and stands to her
honor. She came to Utah in 1863, and crossed the
plains in Rosel Hyde’s company.
		M. L. WOOLSTENHULME, Stake Sec.
SKETCH AND RESOLUTIONS.
	Resolutions adopted by the officers and mem-
bers of the Georgetown Relief Society in honor of,
their beloved sister, Hetta Emeline Welker Dunn,
who departed this life Nov. 17, 1900, at her home
after an illness of many years of lung trouble. 
	Sister Dunn was born in the State of Iowa,


[Column 1]
November 1, 1848. She was a faithful Latter-day
Saint, and as such we mourn her departure. She
was president of the Young Ladies’ Association in
Bloomington (her former home) for ten years, and
second counselor in the Georgetown Relief Society,
which position she filled with honor until her health
failed and she resigned her position as counselor.
	She was of a quiet, retiring disposition, yet
always willing to do her duty. She was the
mother of eight children, three sons and five
daughters, all of whom attended her funeral ex-
cepting one son. who resides in Canada and did
not arrive in time to see his beloved mother, and
drop one tear of sorrow on her marble brow.
	Resolved, That as our Heavenly Father has
taken our beloved sister to her heavenly home,
that we desire to emulate her noble example, and
that we herewith express our heartfelt sympathy
for her bereaved husband and children. Be it
fnrther
	Resolved, That these resolutions be placed on
the record of the society, a copy be sent to the
family and one to the Woman’s Exponent for
publication. 
			SUSAN E. THOMPSON, Pres.
MARY CRAIGG PETERSON.
	Sister Mary Craigg Peterson, who, after an ill-
ness of several months, of dropsy, departed this
life November 11, 1900.
	Sister Peterson was born in Denmark, May 20,
1863. After embracing the Gospel she came to
Zion to mingle with the chosen people of God. She
leaves a husband and four children, three sons and
one daughter the youngest child being a boy six
months old.
	Sister Mary was a faithful worker as a teacher
in the Pleasant Green Ward Relief Society, of Salt
Lake Stake of Zion. She never shrank beneath
the responsibilities of her duties in the cause of
trnth.
	“Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from
henceforth, yea. they rest from their labors and
their works do follow them.”
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Resolved. That as God in His in[--]ite wisdom
saw fit to call our beloved sister to the Great
Beyond, we all share in the grief of her departure
from us, yet joy when we think of her being in
happiness in her heavenly home.
	That we will miss her as a faithful worker and a
dear associate.
	That we present a copy of these resolutions to
her bereaved husband and family, to her mother,
one to the WOMAN’S EXPONENT for publication and
one be placed on file in the Relief Society record
book.
					ALVIRA S. HIRST, Pres.
ANNIE LAZENBY.
	Sister Annie Lazenby died at her home in Loa,
Utah, October 29, 1900. She was the daughter of
[-]ohn Tether and Phebe Oads Tether. She was
born at Thorn, near Doncaster, England, June
30, 1845, joined the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints. June 1866, was baptized by
Elder Fulmer and came to Utah in 1868 on the
good ship Constitution. She crossed the plains
from the Platte River, walking most of the way.
There were sixty wagons drawn by oxen. Cap-
tain John Gallispie’s company arrived in Salt Lake
City in September, where she took a room near
Brother Betson Eardley in the seventh Ward.
	She was married to John T. Lazenby by D. H.
Wells in the Endowment House, March 17, 1869,
and went with her husband to the railroad at the
mouth of Weber canyon on J. W, Young’s con-
tract. They moved to Pleasant Grove June 1869,
and to Minersville Nov. 1869, and came to Fre-
mont Valley Nov. 1878, and located at Loa, which
was then a part of Grass Valley Ward, the in-
habitants at that time being mostly Indians.
	She was a faithful Latter-day Saint, was the
mother of three sons and six daughters. One son
and one daughter preceded her to the better land.
She leaves a husband and two sons (one of whom
is now on a mission to South Carolina) and five
daughters to revere her memory.
	She was a teacher in the Loa Relief Society ever
since its organization until her health was so poor
she could not go as a teacher any longer, but was
always a faithful, energetic woman. She was an
invalid for about five years before her death.
					M. J. TAYLOR, Pres.
ANNA CHRISTINE MARIA PALLISEN JANSEN.
	Sister Anna Christine Maria Pallisen Jansen,
widow of the late A. A. Jansen, of the Third Ward
of Brigham City, died Monday, September 17, at
9:15 p. m., of dropsy, after a lingering illness.

[Column 2]
	She was born October 27, 1838, in Norie, Konges-
lov, Niborg, Amt, Denmark, and joined the Latter-
day Saints when eighteen years of age. She came
to Utah in 1858, settled in Brigham City in 1859.
	She was the mother of four children, two
daughters surviving her.
	She was called and acted as teacher in the Re-
lief Society in 1876 and on March 13, 1878, was
called as second counselor to Mary Stark, which
position she held until she was called to preside
over the Relief Society of the Third Ward, Feb.
25, 1896, which position she held with honor until
a few months before her death, when her failing
health caused her to request and obtain honorable
release.
	She died as she had lived, a true and faithful
Latter-day Saint. Her many friends will miss her,
still to know that her sufferings are passed became
a comfort to those who loved her, and that she is
now enjoying sweet rest.
	The following resolutions were unanimously
adopted at a recent meeting of the Relief Society
of the Third Ward of Brigham City.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, Our Heavenly Father has seen fit in
His infinite love and mercy to call home our be
loved president. Sister Anna Christine Maria
Pallisen Jansen, therefore be it
	Resolved, That in her death we have lost an
earnest and faithful president and worker of the
Relief Society, one who was ever ready to help the
needy and give words of sympathy to those in
trouble.
	That we, her co-laborers, have lost a true, loving
sister, and that we will miss her words of wis-
dom and advice. But we know that God doeth all
things we[-]l, and while we miss her here, she is
happy with her husband and friends in the
eternal world.
	That we express our love and gratitude for
her unceasing labors, present a copy of these
resolutions to her bereaved family, and also that a
copy be sent to the WOMAN’S EXPONENT for publi-
cation and a copy be placed on file in the ward
record book.
			ANNA S. THOMPSON, Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
NEW HALL DEDICATED.
	Minutes of a meeting of the Relief Society
of the Oakley Ward, Cassia Co.. Idaho,
held in the new society hall, the principal
object being the dedication of the building
to the purpose for which it was erected,
President Isabel Martindale presiding.
	Singing by the congregation, “The
happy day is rolling on.” Opening prayer
by Patriarch Franklin Brim. Singing,
“Behold the Savior of mankind.”
	President Martindale felt pleased that the
hall had been so far completed that we could
have it dedicated, and knew that the Lord
had blessed the society in the endeavor
to build a comfortable place fo meet in.
	She then called upon Stake President
Wm. T. Jack to offer the dedicatory prayer.
	President Wm. T. Harper hoped that the
spirit of God would continue with the
meeting as it had to the present. Said
that he sometimes thought that if it were
not for the great mercy of our Heavenly
Father, it would be useles, for us to attempt
to do good, as we so often failed; but God
is full of charity and mercy and is long-
suffering, and is willing to help us as long
as we have a spirit of repentance and are
willing to go before our Father in heaven
and acknowledge our sins and ask Him in
faith for the blessings we need, for the time
is fast coming when we must live by faith,
such faith that we can go before God with-
out doubt, knowing that we will receive
the blessing that we need. It is this power
that is going to make us great on the earth.
Read a few paragraphs from the Doctrine
and Covenants regarding the responsibility
of parents in teaching their children the
principles of the Gospel, and the conse-
quence of a failure on the part of the parents
in Zion in not teaching their children these

[Column 2]
principles, and seeing to it that all children
in Zion are acquainted with the same and
are baptized at the age of eight years. He
urged the young ladies and young men of
Israel to be careful in selecting their life
companion, that they marry only those who
are full of the spirit of the Gospel, that the
fruits of their union may grow up full of
the spirit of God. This condition cannot
be brought about when one of the parents
is opposed to the Church of Jesus Christ.
Asked the blessings of the Lord upon the
sisters especially and the Saints in general,
that we may all feel the importance of living
the lives of Latter-day Saints.
	President Martindale then called upon
Stake President Louisa Haight, of the
Relief Society.
	Sister Haight was glad in meeting with
sisters, and that they had been blessed so
much that they had been able to build so
beautiful a hall, and that it was so nearly
out of debt.
	The Bishop’s counselor, T. W. Robbins,
congratulated the sisters on the haste in
which they had been enabled to complete
their building, and was also greatly inter-
ested in the remarks of President Harper
regarding the responsibility of the parents
in Israel teaching their children the princi-
ples of the Gospel, and the fact that if any
of our children, through our failure to do
these things, should prove untrue to the
principles of truth, that God would require
their sins at our hands, or rather would
visit their sins upon our heads. Asked the
blessing of the Lord upon those who had
labored so hard to complete their building;
also upon all the officers, both ward and
Stake, and upon all that had a desire to
further the kingdom of God.
	Brother George Craner, who had labored
so hard as a member of the building com-
mittee, was called upon to speak a few
words. He referred to the adverse con-
ditions the committee had to meet during
the erection of the building. Said that
there was almost enough money on hand to
settle for the building.
	President Martindale felt pleased with the
spirit manifest during the meeting and for
the many good instructions given by the
brethren. Said that the sisters had always
been willing to do their part in the erection
of the building or any other labor required
of them.
	Brother George Craner proposed a vote of
thanks to Brother George Bunn for the
energy displayed in laying the brick.
Carried.
	Singing, “Come go with me beyond the
sea.” Benediction by Brother George
Bunn.
				J. N. PRICE, Sec. pro tem.


RELIEF SOCIETY ANNUAL REPORTS.
	The time has come for annual reports and
it is important that reports be sent in as soon
as possible after the close of 1900. It is not
only necessary to have Stake reports, but
wherever a Relief Society has been organized
outside Stakes, whether in the United States,
British Empire, Scandinavia, Germany,
Switzerland, Holland, the islands of the sea,
reports for the year 1900, from January 1
to December 31, are expected at head-
quarters.
			E. B. WELLS,
				General Secretary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
ONEIDA STAKE.
	Minutes of the semi-annual conference of
the Relief Society of Oneida Stake, held in
the Preston meeting house, November 10,
1900, President Louisa B. Benson presiding. 
Singing, “We thank Thee, O God, for a
prophet.” Prayer was offered by President
Susan Grant, of Davis County. Singing,
“O, say what is truth.” Roll called, show-
ing, of Stake Board, President Louisa B.
Benson, Second Counselor Hannah Mickle-
sen and Secretary Elise B. Alder. First
Counselor Martha Lawiscli had been called
to Provo on account of death in the family.
Local presidents, ten answered and one sent
a written report.
	Minutes of the morning session of last
conference were read and approved.
	Of the priesthood there were present,
President George C. Parkinson and Brother
Peter Micklesen, of Weston; Bishop George
T. Benson and Brother William Tanner, of
Whitney.
	President Louisa B. Benson asked for the
sympathy and prayers of the sisters. Re-
ported the Stake Board as united, holding
officers’ meetings monthly also hold meet-
ing s quarterly with local officers. “We have
thirteen branches of the Relief Society. At
our last meeting ail the local presidents
were present but three. Since that time I
have met two of these, so that I can report
all the branches of the society but one,
Treasureton, expect to visit that one the
coming Monday. All are doing fairly well.
The attendance at the meetings is not what
we would like it to be, but hope the sisters
will awaken to the necessity of doing their
duty. Some have been very energetic,
having accomplished more work during the
past few months than ever before. This has
especially been the case with Preston
and Franklin. The presidents are ener-
getic and willing to do their duty. There
is a spirit of improvement manifest in our
officers’ meetings. President Susan Grant,
one of the General Board, is with us today,
has kindly come to accompany us in our
tour around the Stake to visit all the
branches. Brother Nuttall was expected,
but owing to circumstances over which he
had no control, had to postpone his visit un-
til next spring. Sister Grant and myself
have visited four branches since we met in
Franklin last Thursday, and we expect to
continue after conference until we have
held meetings with all.”
	Sister Grant said: “It is not good to talk
of our weaknesses, it gives the adversary
power over us. She was pleased to see the
priesthood with us. Had felt blessed in
visiting this Stake so far, had met so many
good brothers and sisters. It is our mission
to administer comfort to our sisters. Teach-
ers should be faithful, so that when visiting
they can stimulate others to faithfulness.
Spoke of the training of children. We
should always be subject to those who are
placed over us. The Prophet Joseph Smith
said that the duty of the Relief Society
was to save souls and to encourage their
husbands in the performance of their duties,

[Column 2]
as well as to look after the poor and afflicted.
Spoke of the law of tithing, it has been
neglected in the past, and the Lord has
been displeased with us. Spoke of the
storing of grain. Felt to reverence the
priesthood of God.
	A vocal solo, “Should you feel inclined
to censure,” was given by Sister Dora
Geddes.
	Counselor Hannah Micklesen expressed
her willingness to do whatever she was
called to do by those who are placed over
her. Thought the Relief Society was a
benefit to all.
	Sister Nellie Parkinson recited, with
much feeling, “A Thread of Thought,”
composed by Sister L. G. Richards.
	President George C. Parkinson was
pleased to be with us. He had enjoyed
the timely remarks of Sister Grant. What
glorious opportunities we have, how
wonderfully we have progressed. Referred
to the beautiful recitation given and en-
couraged mothers to trust in the Lord.
	President L. Benson spoke of the beauties
of motherhood. We have heavy burdens to
bear, but we take up our cross and are
thankful that we have been permitted to
live in this day, if we can only see our
children serving the Lord. The subject of
Sabbath breaking had just come to her
mind. Are we raising Sabbath breakers?
As mothers are we setting an example
worthy of imitation in this respect? Are
we trying to prepare for the Sabbath the
day before, that the Sunday may be a day
of rest and still our children be cared for?
	Meeting adjourned until two p. m. Sing-
ing, “Lo the mighty God appearing.”
Benediction by Brother Peter Mickelson.
	Afternoon session, two o’clock p. m
Singing, “Now let us rejoice.” Prayer by
Brother Joseph Geddes. Singing, “How
firm a foundation.” Minutes of the after-
noon session of last conference were read
and accepted. A circular letter from the
General Beard concerning the conjoint
building to be erected in Salt Lake City for
the Relief Society, Y. L. M. I. A. and
Primary, was read by the secretary. Presi-
dent Benson fully explained the same. The
General and Stake Relief Society officers
were sustained.
	Vocal solo, “Daughters of Zion,” by
Lula B. Parkinson.
	Sister Grant said: “We always have to
depend on the Lord when we speak.” She
had enjoyed the singing. Encouraged the
mothers to teach their children to sing and
told of Apostle Heber J. Grant’s persever-
ance in learning to sing. She had lived in
the time of the Prophet Joseph Smith and
looked forward with pleasure to the time
when she would meet him again and be
recognized by him. Related some instances
of her early life in the Church. Encouraged
the keeping of the Word of Wisdom. Spoke
of and explained the ten cent fund. Gave
an interesting outline of the plans of the
conjoint building, explained its objects and
how much it was needed by the daughters
of Zion. Bishop John Larsen, of Preston,
felt well in meeting with us, found the sis-

[Column 3]
ters to be better Latter-day Saints than the
brethren. He had enjoyed the remarks.
	President Joseph Geddes always enjoyed
hearing the sisters speak, especially those
who had lived in the time of the Prophet
Joseph. This is a work of great magnitude,
the sisters seem awake to their calling.
	Sister Phebe McNeil, President of Fair-
view Relief Society, spoke a short time.
She had been much pleased with the in-
structions we had received during the con-
ference, especially wtth what had been said
in regard to Sabbath breaking.
	Conference was adjourned for six months.
Singing, “Come thou glorious day of
promise.” Benediction by Brother Joseph
Geddes.
			ELISE B. ALDER, Sec.
